title: Journal Entry Practice
description: Given a transaction, complete the double entry. Fill in the account name, and put the amount in the correct Debit or Credit column.

scenarios.scenario: Sold goods to Murphy's Ltd on credit. Invoice 101 — Net: €2,000 + VAT @ 23%.
scenarios.hint: A credit sale creates a debtor (they owe you the full gross amount). The VAT you charged is now owed TO Revenue — it's output VAT, a liability.
scenarios.explanation: Credit sale rule: Debit the Debtor for the gross amount (€2,000 + €460 VAT). Credit Sales for the net amount. Credit VAT Control for the VAT — this is output VAT you now owe Revenue.

scenarios.scenario: Murphy's Ltd pays the full amount owed (€2,460) by bank transfer.
scenarios.hint: The debtor is paying what they owe — the money goes to your bank, and the debtor's balance is cleared.
scenarios.explanation: Receipt from debtor: Debit Bank (asset increases — money received). Credit Debtors (asset decreases — they no longer owe us). This clears the debtor account created in the previous credit sale.

scenarios.scenario: Purchased goods on credit from O'Brien Supplies. Invoice S001 — Net: €1,200 + VAT @ 23%.
scenarios.hint: A credit purchase creates a creditor (you owe them the gross). The VAT you were charged is input VAT — you can reclaim it from Revenue, so it reduces your VAT liability.
scenarios.explanation: Credit purchase: Debit Purchases for the net cost (€1,200). Debit VAT Control for the input VAT (€276) — this reduces what you owe Revenue. Credit Creditors for the full gross amount (€1,476) you now owe the supplier.

scenarios.scenario: Paid O'Brien Supplies the full amount owed (€1,476) by bank transfer.
scenarios.hint: You're clearing a creditor — the liability goes down, and so does your bank balance.
scenarios.explanation: Payment to creditor: Debit Creditors (liability decreases — we no longer owe them). Credit Bank (asset decreases — money leaves the bank). This clears the creditor account created in the credit purchase.

scenarios.scenario: Paid weekly wages of €2,400 by bank transfer.
scenarios.hint: Wages are an expense — they increase expenses (debit) and reduce your bank balance (credit).
scenarios.explanation: Wages payment: Debit Wages (expense increases). Credit Bank (asset decreases — money leaves bank). Note: in practice, PAYE/PRSI deductions are held in separate liability accounts, but for this basic entry we record the gross wages.

scenarios.scenario: Purchased new machinery for the business: Net €5,000 + VAT @ 23% = €6,150. Paid by cheque.
scenarios.hint: Buying a fixed asset: the asset account increases (debit). You can reclaim the VAT (debit VAT Control). The bank decreases (credit).
scenarios.explanation: Fixed asset purchase: Debit the asset account (Machinery) for the net cost €5,000 — this is what the asset is worth. Debit VAT Control €1,150 as reclaimable input VAT. Credit Bank €6,150 for the gross amount paid.

scenarios.scenario: The monthly bank statement shows a bank charge of €55 that has not yet been entered in the cash book.
scenarios.hint: Bank charges are an expense. They appear on the bank statement, so the bank balance has already decreased — you just need to record it in the books.
scenarios.explanation: Bank charges: Debit Bank Charges (expense increases — the charge is a cost). Credit Bank (asset decreases — the bank took the money). This is a common bank reconciliation item — charges appear on the statement before you record them.

scenarios.scenario: A cash sale was made to a walk-in customer: Net €600 + VAT @ 23% = €738. Paid directly into the bank.
scenarios.hint: Cash sales bypass the Sales Day Book. The money goes straight to the bank. Don't forget the output VAT.
scenarios.explanation: Cash sale: Debit Bank €738 (the gross amount received goes into the bank). Credit Sales €600 (income earned is the net amount). Credit VAT Control €138 (output VAT on the sale, owed to Revenue).

scenarios.scenario: Paid stationery expenses from petty cash: €18.
scenarios.hint: Petty cash payments: the expense goes up (debit) and the petty cash float goes down (credit).
scenarios.explanation: Petty cash expense: Debit Stationery €18 (expense increases). Credit Petty Cash €18 (the float decreases). Petty cash is a small asset — when you spend from it, the asset decreases (credit). Petty cash items are typically under the VAT registration threshold or exempt.

scenarios.scenario: The owner introduces €10,000 of their own money into the business bank account as additional capital.
scenarios.hint: Capital introduced: the business has more money (bank increases) and owes more to the owner (capital increases).
scenarios.explanation: Capital introduction: Debit Bank €10,000 (asset increases — money received into the business bank). Credit Capital €10,000 (liability to owner increases — the owner has invested more). Capital is what the business owes back to the owner.
